title: Quiz Design for Screen Readers and Keyboards
learningObjectives: Identify quiz question types that work well with assistive technology
Avoid question types and patterns that create barriers (drag-and-drop, image-based matching without alt text, timed auto-submit)
Build accessible quizzes in Canvas and/or Blackboard using keyboard-friendly question types

keyTerms.definition: The ability to use a quiz or interactive system using only the keyboard (Tab to move forward, Shift+Tab to move backward, Enter or Space to select). Essential for users who cannot use a mouse, including those with motor disabilities or using screen readers.

An interaction pattern where users click and hold an object, then drag it to a new location and release. This violates WCAG 2.2 criterion 2.5.7 (Dragging Movements) because there is no keyboard-accessible alternative. Common in matching quizzes and sorting exercises.

The system's ability to show the user where they are in a form or interface — typically a visible outline or highlight around the active element. Poor focus visibility makes keyboard navigation confusing for all users, especially those with cognitive disabilities.

A comparison table showing which quiz question types (multiple choice, dropdown, true/false, short answer, essay, hotspot, drag-and-drop, image-based matching) are compatible with keyboard navigation and screen readers.
A quiz question that asks the user to click on a region of an image (e.g., 'Click the spinal cord in this diagram'). Usually not keyboard accessible unless the LMS provides a text-based alternative.

contentBlocks.title: Which Question Types Work?
contentBlocks.body:
Not all quiz question types are created equal when it comes to accessibility. When you build a quiz, your choice of question type directly affects whether students using screen readers or keyboard-only navigation can answer it.

Here is what typically works:

**Question types that are generally accessible:**

- **Multiple choice** — Students can Tab through options and select with Enter or Space. Make sure each option is a separate, labeled choice (not nested in a table or with unclear focus).
- **True/False** — Same accessibility as multiple choice. Simple and reliable.
- **Short answer/Essay** — Students can Tab into the text field and type. No interaction barriers.
- **Dropdown/Select** — Students Tab to the dropdown, press Enter or Space to open, arrow keys to select, and Enter to confirm. This works well in most LMS systems.
- **Checkboxes** — Multiple checkboxes that students can Tab through and toggle with Space key. Provides the same functionality as multiple choice but with all options visible (more accessible than dropdowns).

**Question types that often create barriers:**

- **Drag-and-drop** — No keyboard alternative exists. A student using Tab cannot access this. Violates WCAG 2.2 criterion 2.5.7 (Dragging Movements).
- **Hotspot/image-based matching** — Requires clicking a specific region on an image. Keyboard-only users cannot do this. Screen reader users cannot hear which region is active.
- **Fill-in-the-blank with text boxes** — Depends on how it's implemented. If the fill-in spots are labeled (e.g., "Blank 1: [____]"), it can work. If they're unlabeled or visually placed, it fails.
- **Image-based matching without alt text** — If the quiz shows images to match (e.g., "Match the photo to the label"), but the images have no alt text, screen reader users cannot identify which image is which.

The good news: the question types that work well are the ones you probably use most often. The ones that create barriers are the "fancy" interactive types that can seem engaging but actually exclude students.

contentBlocks.title: Question Type Accessibility Matrix
contentBlocks.imageAlt: Matrix showing how common quiz question types perform on keyboard, screen reader, and extended-time accommodations. Multiple choice, true or false, short answer, essay, dropdown matching, and button-based ordering are fully accessible. Fill-in-the-blank is mostly accessible with caveats. Drag-and-drop, hotspot or image-click, and drag-based ordering fail keyboard and should be replaced with alternatives.
contentBlocks.imageCaption: Red = replace. Amber = verify in your LMS. Green = safe to use.

contentBlocks.title: The Same Assessment, Two Ways
contentBlocks.body:
Let's say you want to assess whether students can match five vocabulary terms to their definitions. Here is how the same assessment plays out in two different formats.

**Version 1: Drag-and-Drop Matching (Not Accessible)**

You create a Canvas matching question. The left side shows five terms (Word1, Word2, Word3, Word4, Word5). The right side shows five definitions. Students drag each term to match its definition. Looks great on screen. Takes about 2 minutes for a sighted student with a mouse.

Now imagine a student using a screen reader. They Tab into the quiz. The screen reader announces: "Matching question. Drag the term on the left to the definition on the right." The student presses Tab to navigate the terms — they can hear them listed: "Word1, Word2, Word3, Word4, Word5." But there is no way to interact with them. Tab goes to the next question. They get no score for this question. They move on, frustrated.

**Version 2: Dropdown Matching (Accessible)**

You rebuild the same assessment. Instead of dragging, you create five separate multiple-choice questions:

- Q1: "What does Word1 mean?" with options A, B, C, D (the five definitions)
- Q2: "What does Word2 mean?" (same options, different correct answer)
- Q3–Q5: Same pattern.

The assessment tests identical knowledge (students still must match each term to its definition). The screen reader user can now navigate: Tab to each question, use arrow keys to select the definition they think is correct, press Enter to confirm. All five questions answered. Score recorded correctly.

Both versions test the same skill. One excludes students; one includes them. The second takes slightly more space in the quiz, but the difference is negligible.

**In Canvas specifically:** You would avoid the "Matching" question type entirely and instead use "Multiple Choice" questions, one per term.

**In Blackboard:** You would avoid "Hot Spot" and "Matching" questions and use "Multiple Choice" instead.

contentBlocks.title: Keyboard Navigation and Focus
contentBlocks.body:
Even when you use "accessible" question types, the quiz still needs to work with keyboard-only navigation. This means:

1. **Tab order works correctly.** When a student presses Tab, focus moves forward through the quiz in a logical order — first question to last. When they press Shift+Tab, focus moves backward.

2. **Focus is visible.** When focus moves to a button, link, or text field, there is a visible indicator (usually an outline or highlight). Do not disable focus indicators in your CSS or style settings — they are essential for keyboard users.

3. **Enter and Space work.** When a student has focus on a button or radio button, pressing Enter or Space activates it. This should require no mouse click.

4. **Dropdown keys work.** When a student Tab-s into a dropdown menu, pressing Enter opens it, arrow keys navigate options, and pressing Enter selects. This is standard and usually works in modern LMS systems, but it is worth testing.

Here is a simple 2-minute test you can run on any quiz you create:

**The Keyboard-Only Test:**

1. Open your quiz in a new browser tab.
2. Put your mouse away — do not touch it.
3. Press Tab to move through the quiz. Can you reach every question?
4. For each question type, try answering using only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, and arrow keys.
5. If you get stuck, that is a sign the question type is not keyboard accessible.

Do this test before publishing the quiz. If students tell you later that they could not navigate your quiz with a keyboard, you now know why — and you can fix it by swapping out the question type.

Multiple choice, true/false, short answer, dropdown, and checkbox questions work well with screen readers and keyboard navigation. Drag-and-drop, hotspot, and image-based matching do not. Test every quiz with keyboard-only navigation before publishing. If you get stuck during the test, your students will too.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Open one of your existing quizzes in your LMS (Canvas, Blackboard, or other). Try navigating it using only your keyboard — Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, and arrow keys. No mouse. Report what happened: Could you complete every question? Where did you get stuck? What question types caused problems, and what could you change them to?
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response describes a specific, real quiz the faculty member actually tested (not hypothetical). It identifies at least one question type that worked and at least one that didn't. It demonstrates understanding of the keyboard navigation challenge and suggests concrete replacements (e.g., 'replace the drag-and-drop with a dropdown'). It reflects on what the experience revealed about accessibility.
